data.title: Valley National Bancorp refines 2025 guidance with 8–10% net interest income growth target while advancing specialty verticals
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Valley National Bancorp (VLY) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Ira D. Robbins, President, CEO & Chairman, reported net income of $133 million or $0.22 per diluted share and adjusted net income of $134 million or $0.23 per share, noting sequential growth in adjusted earnings and stating, "Our profitability ratios, including return on average assets and return on tangible shareholders' equity continue to trend higher and are on track to meet the full year guidance that we outlined this past January."
  • Robbins emphasized strategic imperatives: "Over the past 12 months, we have added over 105,000 new deposit accounts, which has contributed to approximately 8% core deposit growth. As a result, our reliance on indirect deposits has declined from 18% down to 13%." He highlighted a 51 basis point reduction in average cost of deposits compared to the prior year.
  • Robbins discussed commercial loan diversification: “Since 2017, we have grown our C&I portfolio at a 19% compound annual rate, including nearly 15% growth over the last 12 months.” He pointed to specialty verticals like health care and fund finance, adding, “We have never -- I'll repeat never taken a loss on any Valley originated health care C&I loans over this 20-year period.”
  • Fee income growth was also addressed, as Robbins stated, “Noninterest income has grown at a 12% annual rate since 2017, more than double the pace of our peers.”
  • Robbins noted, “We have grown cumulative tangible book value with dividends over 105% during my tenure as CEO. This is approximately 15% greater than the peer medium.”
  • Travis P. Lan, Senior Executive VP & CFO, provided financial highlights: "We continue to expect approximately 3% loan growth for the year, consistent with our prior update. Given the loan growth is trending toward the lower end of our original guidance, we are refining our net interest income growth estimate to a range of 8% to 10%. Our outlook for noninterest income remains unchanged at 6% to 10% growth. We are lowering our noninterest expense growth guidance to a range of 2% to 4%."

Outlook

  • Lan confirmed the company maintains its expectation for approximately 3% loan growth for 2025, but noted loan growth is trending toward the lower end of the original guidance.
  • Net interest income growth is now estimated in the range of 8% to 10% for the year, refined from prior guidance.
  • Noninterest income growth remains unchanged at 6% to 10%.
  • Noninterest expense growth guidance has been lowered to 2% to 4%.
  • Lan stated, “In aggregate, these modest directional adjustments are expected to result in full year earnings per share that remains broadly in line with current consensus estimates.”
  • Credit guidance now anticipates net charge-offs of $100 million to $125 million and a provision estimate of approximately $150 million for the full year.

Financial Results

  • Core customer deposit growth was reported at $600 million for the quarter, with deposit beta during the recent rate decrease cycle at 51%.
  • Gross loans increased at an annual pace of 6%, with strong growth in C&I and indirect auto lending. Fund finance and health care accounted for roughly 60% of the quarter's net growth in C&I.
  • The CRE concentration ratio declined to 349% from 474% at the end of 2023.
  • Net interest income increased 3% sequentially, marking the fifth consecutive quarter of NIM improvement.
  • Noninterest income growth was driven by increased swap volumes tied to CRE originations and growth in FX and syndication fees. Deposit service charges rose as well.
  • Adjusted noninterest expenses grew modestly due to merit-based salary increases and higher incentive accruals, but the efficiency ratio improved to 55.2%.
  • Tangible book value increased, and while the total risk-based capital ratio declined due to redemption of subordinated debt, other regulatory capital ratios improved.

Q&A

  • Christopher Edward McGratty, KBW: Asked about margin expansion and deposit pricing. Lan responded, "I think we still anticipate the margin will increase as the year goes on and then into 2026 as well...This quarter, we added over $1 billion of new deposits at a blended rate of 2.77%."
  • McGratty inquired about charge-off guidance and new nonaccruals. Mark Saeger explained, "We point to stabilization on nonaccruals that we saw this quarter...the guidance we had given was an expectation for charge-offs and reserve levels to provision levels to be higher at the beginning of the year, and that's consistent now with our guidance that we're showing through the end of the year."
  • David Charles Smith, Truist Securities: Sought commentary on technology sector banking and competitive landscape. Robbins shared, “Right now, I think they have well over 50% of the market share, if anything, from Israel coming to the United States really goes through Valley. So a real strong connectivity there, but really an infrastructure that we can leverage.”
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Asked about C&I loan growth sources. Robbins noted a “30% higher” C&I pipeline and highlighted the bank’s focus on small to midsize businesses.
  • Matthew M. Breese, Stephens Inc.: Questioned deposit costs and the potential for a buyback. Robbins stated, "I think we have a lot more flexibility today than we've ever had in my time being at Valley...That said, I think there are organic growth opportunities for us."
  • Jonathan Rau, Barclays: Inquired about competitive sentiment in New Jersey and CRE runoff impact. Lan responded, "As we head into 2026, I don't anticipate CRE will be a headwind from a dollar perspective."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly positive tone, with questions focused on margin expansion, deposit pricing, technology sector growth, and credit quality. There was limited skepticism, with most analysts seeking clarification or more detail rather than expressing deep concern.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing strategic execution and improvement in core metrics. During Q&A, management responses were detailed and constructive, with Robbins and Lan both highlighting ongoing momentum and risk controls. Robbins stated, "We do see a pretty clear path to get to those numbers."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management demonstrated increased confidence, especially around specialty verticals and deposit transformation. Analyst sentiment was largely consistent quarter-over-quarter, with a focus on operational execution and guidance clarity.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan growth remains at 3% for 2025, but the net interest income growth estimate was refined from a previous 9%–12% range to 8%–10%.
  • CRE concentration ratio dropped further, now at 349% from 353% in Q1 and 474% at the end of 2023, surpassing the year-end target.
  • Core deposit growth and reduction in indirect deposit reliance have accelerated, as highlighted by management.
  • Management’s confidence in specialty verticals (health care, fund finance, technology) increased, with more detailed commentary on their risk-adjusted returns and growth.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted more toward deposit mix, competitive pressures, and the scalability of fee income businesses, in addition to ongoing CRE risk and loan growth.
  • Management’s tone was more optimistic, citing clear visibility into reaching key performance targets and a “disconnect between the quality of our franchise and the valuation of our shares.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited competitive deposit pricing as a continued challenge, particularly in the New York market, but expressed confidence in strategic initiatives to mitigate this.
  • Analysts raised concerns about CRE runoff, loan growth sustainability, and credit quality, particularly regarding delinquencies and nonaccruals, which management addressed with detail and cited specific loan resolutions.
  • Management continues to monitor the impact of political developments (e.g., New York mayoral race) on regulated multifamily portfolios but indicated limited exposure and strong underwriting practices.

Final Takeaway

Valley National Bancorp highlighted sequential improvement in profitability and efficiency, backed by robust C&I loan growth, deposit transformation, and expansion of specialty verticals. Management refined guidance for net interest income and noninterest expenses while reiterating confidence in achieving performance targets and further reducing risk concentrations. The consistent execution of strategic initiatives, combined with the scalability of fee income streams and a strong capital position, positions the company for continued momentum and a narrowing of the gap between franchise quality and share valuation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Honeywell raises full-year EPS guidance to $10.45–$10.65 while advancing portfolio transformation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Honeywell International Inc. (HON) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Vimal M. Kapur reported that "Honeywell again delivered solid results in the second quarter, meeting or exceeding all our financial commitments in a time of significant global economic change." He highlighted accelerated organic sales and orders growth, attributing these to "consistent spending and execution on new product development across our businesses." Kapur announced a raised sales and earnings guidance for the full year, integrating "all currently known tariffs and the uncertain business conditions going forward."
  • Kapur detailed strategic progress on Honeywell’s planned separation into three public companies, stating, "We continue to make great progress along with the path to separate into 3 independent companies, which we believe will maximize long-term value for all Honeywell stakeholders."
  • Kapur announced the intention to pursue strategic alternatives for the Productivity Solutions and Services (PSS) and Warehouse and Workflow Solutions businesses, noting that "the results of this pursuit... will clarify the standalone automation company's go-forward strategy and value proposition."
  • CFO Mike Stepniak stated, "In the second quarter, we built upon a strong start to the year as we again exceeded our guidance for organic sales growth and adjusted earnings per share." Stepniak noted that "segment profit expanded 8% from the prior year, in line with sales, and segment margin finished nearly flat and within our guidance range."
  • Stepniak highlighted $10.5 billion in orders for the quarter, up 6% year-over-year, and a record backlog of $36.6 billion.

Outlook

  • Stepniak reported that Honeywell is raising the lower end of its full year organic sales growth guidance range by 200 basis points, now projecting growth of 4% to 5% for the year, or 3% to 4% excluding the prior year impact from the Bombardier agreement.
  • Full year sales are now projected to be $40.8 billion to $41.3 billion, driven by better organic growth, foreign currency tailwinds, and additional revenue from the Sundyne acquisition.
  • The company expects full year earnings per share of $10.45 to $10.65, up 6% to 8%.
  • Third quarter organic sales are expected to reach $10 billion to $10.3 billion, with organic sales growth of 2% to 4%.
  • Segment margin for the full year is expected to be up 40 to 60 basis points, or down 30 to 10 basis points excluding Bombardier.

Financial Results

  • Stepniak reported that earnings per share in the second quarter was $2.45 per share, while adjusted earnings per share was $2.75 per share, up 10% year-over-year.
  • Second quarter sales grew 5% organically, with Defense and Space and UOP leading growth with double-digit performance.
  • Backlog grew 10% organically to a record $36.6 billion.
  • Free cash flow in the second quarter was $1 billion, down by about $100 million from the prior year due to tariff-related cost inflation and expanded capital project spending.
  • The company allocated $2.2 billion for the Sundyne acquisition and returned over $2.4 billion to shareholders in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Julian C.H. Mitchell, Barclays Bank: Asked about Aerospace supply chain and margin trajectory. Stepniak responded that "orders in Aerospace, extremely strong, continue to be strong on all fronts," explaining that recent commercial OE destocking is transitory and margins are expected to improve in the second half. Kapur added, "these issues are transitionary, and we have very high confidence on the Aero margin projections we have laid out."
  • Andrew Burris Obin, BofA Securities: Inquired about UOP verticals and second half outlook. Kapur explained that strong Q2 growth was due to a "big licensing agreement with a customer" and higher catalyst sales, but energy project spend is "moving more to the right" due to economic uncertainty.
  • Nigel Edward Coe, Wolfe Research: Asked about energy project timing and Aero inflation. Kapur said LNG remains "very strong," but sustainable fuels projects are delayed. Stepniak clarified that passing tariffs in Aerospace takes longer due to contract structures.
  • Scott Reed Davis, Melius Research: Questioned Quantinuum and R&D spending. Kapur confirmed commitment to deconsolidate, with IPO readiness targeted for end of '27, and emphasized the strategic rationale for elevated R&D: "it's really preparing Honeywell for the future for the better organic growth because we are putting bets on the right spots."
  • Sheila Karin Kahyaoglu, Jefferies LLC: Asked about Aerospace aftermarket and OE trends. Stepniak said aftermarket is "normalizing" and that OE destocking is "predominantly...from North America platforms," expected to normalize by Q4.
  • Christopher M. Snyder, Morgan Stanley: Asked about portfolio actions and the decision to review Warehouse Automation. Kapur stated, "we have completed the process" of portfolio review, and the decision is based on end market growth rates and fit.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on the sustainability of margin projections, energy project timing, and the impact of tariffs, reflecting a neutral to slightly cautious tone.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently emphasizing transitory challenges and strategic readiness. Kapur used phrases such as "we are confident" and "we have very high confidence" regarding future margins and growth.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management appear incrementally more focused on navigating macro uncertainty, but management’s tone remains consistently confident.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw an explicit increase in full-year sales and EPS guidance, compared to the previous quarter’s more cautious stance.
  • Portfolio transformation advanced, with strategic alternatives announced for PSS and Warehouse Automation, following ongoing separation plans outlined in Q1.
  • R&D investment as a percentage of sales rose to 4.6% from 4.5% in Q1, with management continuing to stress its importance for future growth.
  • Free cash flow was slightly lower than Q1, attributed to higher tariffs and capital spending.
  • Management’s confidence in mitigation strategies and separation execution has increased, with more concrete timelines for spin-offs provided.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted tariff-related cost inflation and delayed energy projects as key headwinds.
  • Stepniak noted, "capital project spending expanded as planned," and that tariff-related inventory inflation pushed down free cash flow.
  • Orders in certain sectors, particularly energy projects and catalyst sales, are subject to macroeconomic and legislative uncertainty, with some demand shifting into 2026.
  • Analyst concerns focused on the sustainability of margins, potential delays in portfolio actions, and the impact of tariffs and economic uncertainty on demand.

Final Takeaway

Honeywell’s second quarter featured accelerated organic growth and record backlog, prompting management to raise full-year sales and earnings guidance. The company is advancing its separation into three public companies, pursuing strategic alternatives for selected businesses, and investing in R&D to drive future growth. Management emphasized confidence in overcoming macro and tariff-related challenges through proactive mitigation and productivity measures, while signaling continued focus on high-return capital deployment and portfolio optimization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oceaneering narrows 2025 EBITDA guidance to $390M–$420M as ROV pricing and ADTech contracts drive outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Oceaneering International, Inc. (OII) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Roderick A. Larson, President and CEO, highlighted that "When we provided our adjusted EBITDA guidance during the first quarter earnings call, we projected a quarterly year-over-year increase of 16%. We delivered a 20% increase in consolidated adjusted EBITDA." He emphasized consistency with "8 straight quarters of meeting or exceeding our adjusted EBITDA guidance range and 12 quarters out of the last 13."
  • Larson noted that "all of our operating segments contributed to beating the midpoint of our guidance by delivering quarterly year-over-year improvements in revenue, operating income and operating income margin." ADTech's improved results were attributed to new contract awards, while OPG saw margin expansion due to international well intervention projects. Manufactured Products reported higher-margin backlog conversion, and ROV revenue per day reached $11,265, inflecting earlier than expected.
  • Larson reported consolidated net income of $54.4 million or $0.54 per share, with consolidated revenue at $698 million, a 4% increase over the prior year quarter. Consolidated operating income rose to $79.2 million and adjusted EBITDA grew to $103 million. Free cash flow reached $46.9 million, and the company ended the quarter with $434 million in cash and no borrowings under its credit facility.
  • By segment, SSR operating income improved to $64.5 million and EBITDA margin expanded to 35%. ROV fleet utilization was 67%, and average revenue per day utilized increased. Manufactured Products operating income increased to $18.8 million, with a 31% rise and a 4% revenue increase to $145 million. OPG operating income improved to $21.7 million, with margin expansion to 15%. ADTech operating income increased 125% to $16.3 million on a 13% revenue increase. Larson also highlighted $100 million in order commitments secured early in Q3 for Manufactured Products.
  • Larson stated, "We appreciate everyone's continued interest in Oceaneering, and I'll be happy to take any questions you may have."
  • CFO Alan R. Curtis explained, "We do have line of sight to a good amount of it because it's sitting in receivables. So I think it's going and getting paid for the work we've performed and bringing that cash in, in Q3, Q4 time frame."

Outlook

  • The company projects Q3 2025 consolidated EBITDA in the range of $100 million to $110 million.
  • For the full year 2025, Oceaneering narrowed guidance for adjusted EBITDA to $390 million to $420 million, with consolidated revenue expected to grow in the mid-single-digit percentage range.
  • By segment, SSR is forecast to improve operating results on a mid-single-digit percentage revenue increase, with EBITDA margin projected in the mid-30% range. Manufactured Products and ADTech are both expected to deliver significantly improved operating results on increased revenue, with ADTech operating income margin anticipated in the low teens percentage range. OPG results are expected to improve on flat to slightly increased revenue, but Q4 activity is expected to be lower than Q4 2024.
  • Larson noted, "We foresee continued growth beyond 2025, driven by improved visibility into an increasing number of contracted floating rigs in the second half of 2026, sustained progression in ROE revenue per day utilized, anticipated higher levels of FIDs, expectations for supportive oil prices, our ability to perform and offer more life-of-field services, increased demand and geographic expansion for our ADTech business and increased market demand for our Mobile Robotics Technologies."

Financial Results

  • Consolidated revenue for Q2 2025 was $698 million, up 4% year-over-year.
  • Net income reached $54.4 million or $0.54 per share, and consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $103 million, a 20% increase.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $46.9 million, and the company reported $434 million in cash with no borrowings.
  • SSR operating income was $64.5 million, Manufactured Products $18.8 million, OPG $21.7 million, and ADTech $16.3 million, with all segments showing year-over-year improvements.
  • The company repurchased $10 million in shares for the fourth consecutive quarter.

Q&A

  • Eddie Kim, Barclays: Asked about the impact of offshore rig white space on the business, specifically on the ROV segment. Roderick A. Larson replied, "We were expecting to get closer to that exiting the year with a 70% overall utilization for ROV. So we've seen some of it, as we talked about, getting to our pricing point sooner than we expected is offset some of that. ... But really, I mean, the thing to watch in the SSR business is going to be Survey."
  • Kim also inquired about orders in Manufactured Products. Larson responded that "orders year-over-year, we think are more '24 to '25 are more flattish. ... I mentioned earlier, $100 million already in the first few weeks."
  • David Christopher Smith, Pickering Energy Partners Insights: Asked about the ROV utilization outlook and whether the lower guidance was related to vessel or rig support. Larson said, "It's both. ... We're just being, I think, conservative to not overestimate what could come to be in the fourth quarter."
  • Smith followed up on ROV pricing, asking if performance-based deals or FX were factors. Larson replied, "I would say it's mostly the contracts. ... there's not a big FX effect, and there's not a big effect of the performance- based pricing yet."
  • Smith asked about the ramp in free cash flow for the second half. Curtis responded, "A lot of it is kind of how we've seen the last 4 or 5 years play out where Q1 is a pretty big cash draw for us. ... We do have line of sight to a good amount of it because it's sitting in receivables."
  • Joshua W. Jayne, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about visibility and booking in OPG. Larson stated, "It's a little bit of function of when -- I've always talked about these bigger chunks of business when we start to book like the BP Mauritania, those big international contracts really help our visibility a lot."
  • Jayne also questioned the impact of the 'Big Beautiful Bill.' Larson explained, "the OTECH side with the vehicle business is a big thing ... But probably even more exciting is the way it affects the other 2 businesses ... we see things like Artemis going to the moon, those things actually being refunded. ... So we are already thinking about how do we gear up to have more capacity to serve that market."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly positive, with questions focused on segment outlooks, cash flow visibility, and orders, but no strongly negative challenges.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone, emphasizing consistency, execution, and growth opportunities, with phrases such as "We remain positive about both our Energy and Aerospace and Defense markets that we serve, and we're confident in the value we deliver to our customers."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone showed increased confidence, particularly in narrowing guidance and order commitments, while analysts remained steady in their focus on execution and upcoming risks.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reported higher net income, revenue, and adjusted EBITDA compared to Q1 2025.
  • Guidance narrowed for full year EBITDA to $390 million to $420 million, compared to the previous range of $380 million to $430 million.
  • ROV revenue per day increased and inflected earlier than expected. ADTech showed a sharp increase in operating income due to contract ramp-up.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on ROV utilization and pricing, free cash flow, and orders, but Q2 questions included more detail on government contract impacts and future capacity expansion.
  • Management tone was more assured in Q2, with increased visibility on order intake and cash flow.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged potential impacts from offshore rig white space, noting that increased abandonment activity in Europe may offset lower rig support.
  • There is a risk in the SSR Survey segment if new opportunities do not materialize, with possible cold stacking of a survey vessel.
  • OPG is expected to see a shift toward lower-margin IMR work in the U.S. Gulf, which could impact results in the second half of the year.
  • Manufactured Products faced muted bookings in the first half, but management expects order intake to be back half loaded, with $100 million already secured early in Q3.
  • ADTech depends on continued government funding and contract ramp-up, with management monitoring the impact of new legislation and defense budgets.

Final Takeaway

Oceaneering delivered strong Q2 results across all segments, with earlier-than-expected ROV pricing gains and a substantial ramp in ADTech operating income from recent contract awards. The company narrowed its full year 2025 adjusted EBITDA guidance to $390 million–$420 million, citing increased order visibility and ongoing contract execution. Management remains confident in both energy and defense end markets, with solid cash generation, a robust order book, and expectations for continued growth through 2025 and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Waste Connections outlines $9.45B 2025 revenue target while advancing $200M in acquisitions and share repurchases
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Waste Connections (WCN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ronald J. Mittelstaedt highlighted that Waste Connections "delivered results above the high end of our outlook for the quarter" despite "incremental headwinds in Q2 from lower-than-expected contributions from higher-margin, commodity-related activities and continued sluggishness in the economy along with tariff-induced uncertainties." He noted the completion of approximately $200 million in annualized revenue from acquisitions so far in 2025, and a robust pipeline for further deals. Share repurchases have also begun, reflecting the company's flexibility for "increased return of capital to shareholders including through opportunistic share repurchases, which are already underway."
  • Mittelstaedt reiterated that the full year 2025 outlook remains unchanged, providing for "approximately 6% revenue growth and 50 basis points of adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to 33%."
  • He emphasized operational improvements, stating, "voluntary turnover once again stepped down sequentially, marking our 11th consecutive quarter of improvement...safety results, which are highly correlated to turnover, once again hit new historic lows."
  • CFO Mary Anne Whitney reported, "In the second quarter, revenue of $2.407 billion exceeded the high end of our outlook and was up $159 million or 7.1% year-over-year. Acquisitions completed since the year ago period contributed about $113 million net of divestitures."
  • Mittelstaedt announced the retirement of Executive Vice President and COO Darrell Chambliss, effective August 8, 2025, with further leadership announcements forthcoming.

Outlook

  • Whitney stated, "Given the strength of our performance in the first half of the year and updating for recent commodity values and RINs and acquisitions completed to date, we are maintaining our full year 2025 outlook as provided in February as follows: Revenue is estimated at approximately $9.45 billion."
  • Adjusted EBITDA is targeted at approximately $3.12 billion or 33%, "again within the range of our February outlook in spite of that mix shift."
  • The outlook excludes any impact from additional acquisitions that may close during the remainder of the year.
  • For Q3 and Q4, Whitney indicated, "the adjusted EBITDA margin is expected to average 33.6% in the back half of the year, up about 60 basis points year-over-year."

Financial Results

  • Q2 revenue reached $2.407 billion, up $159 million or 7.1% year-over-year, with core pricing of 6.6%.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q2 was $786.4 million, up 7.5% year-over-year, with a margin of 32.7%.
  • Year-to-date, adjusted free cash flow was $699 million on capital expenditures, up over $110 million year-over-year.
  • Share repurchases totaled over $240 million year-to-date, with 1.3 million shares bought back.
  • The company completed a $500 million public offering in senior notes to diversify funding and ended the quarter with debt of approximately $8.35 billion and liquidity over $1.1 billion.

Q&A

  • Tyler Brown, Raymond James: Asked if large share repurchases signal a shift in capital allocation or M&A strategy. Mittelstaedt responded, "It is not a change in capital allocation strategy whatsoever. It's just that we have the capacity to do both."
  • Brown followed up on expected M&A impact for 2025. Whitney explained, "the acquisition contribution from the previous year's rollover and deals done had been $300 million, and that stepped up by $75 million with the incremental closing of $125 million during the course of the first 2 quarters."
  • Brown inquired about E&P revenue sustainability. Whitney replied, "it's between $160 million and $170 million. I mean depending on seasonality, it's -- I think you got to meter that a little bit."
  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: Probed the volume shedding dynamic. Whitney stated, "the most negative quarter would therefore be Q3, because you have the combined impact of those ongoing impacts plus...lower seasonal ramp impacting the revenue growth Q3 versus Q2."
  • Noah Duke Kaye, Oppenheimer: Asked about revenue mix changes. Whitney detailed, "$75 million incremental in acquisition contribution, a little bit of a good guide from FX...the offset are primarily those commodity-driven reductions."
  • Kevin Chiang, CIBC: Asked about margin improvements from better retention and safety. Whitney said, "we're north of that, maybe 60 to 70 basis points in terms of good guys that we're seeing."
  • Bryan Burgmeier, Citi: Sought clarity on construction-driven volume headwinds. Whitney noted, "Q2 was the seventh quarter in a row for [C&D tons] being down year-over-year, and it's averaged between 7% and 8% Q1 to Q2."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly cautious tone, pressing for clarity on capital allocation, volume trends, and the sustainability of margin improvements, with Kaplan and Kaye probing potential downside risks and timing of recovery.
  • Management’s sentiment remained confident in prepared remarks and Q&A, with Mittelstaedt emphasizing, "We are extremely pleased with our second quarter results, which reflect the enduring strength and consistency of solid waste regardless of the economic environment," but also displaying caution in maintaining guidance: "In a more normalized environment...we would have prompted us to raise our full year guidance. However, given the uncertainty...we view maintaining our 2025 guidance as prudent and as a win."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone remained steady but slightly more defensive in the face of persistent volume headwinds and commodity pricing weakness, while analysts continued to focus on volume and margin sustainability.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company maintained its full year 2025 outlook for revenue and adjusted EBITDA, unchanged from the previous quarter.
  • Management reiterated a focus on margin expansion, highlighting 70 basis points underlying solid waste margin expansion in Q2, similar to Q1.
  • The Q2 call featured more discussion on the impact of commodity weakness and ongoing volume shedding, as well as capital allocation flexibility between M&A and share repurchases.
  • The retirement of COO Darrell Chambliss was announced, marking a notable management change not present in the Q1 call.
  • Analysts’ questions continued to focus on volume trends, margin sustainability, and capital deployment, with a recurring emphasis on the timing and magnitude of volume recovery.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "incremental and growing headwinds" from weaker commodity-related activities, sluggish macro environment, and tariff-induced uncertainties.
  • Specific challenges included ongoing volume declines in construction and demolition waste, lower commodity prices (OCC and RINs), and volatility in U.S. E&P waste activity.
  • Shedding of underperforming contracts and the closure of Chiquita Canyon landfill continued to weigh on volumes.
  • Remediation at Chiquita Canyon landfill is progressing, with management pointing to regulatory complexity and the need for EPA involvement to streamline oversight and reduce costs.
  • Management acknowledged risk management cost reductions are "lagging and remained a headwind in the quarter."

Final Takeaway

Waste Connections is maintaining its full-year 2025 outlook, projecting approximately $9.45 billion in revenue and $3.12 billion in adjusted EBITDA, despite persistent headwinds from commodity price declines, subdued construction activity, and ongoing purposeful contract shedding. The company is advancing a robust acquisition strategy, having already completed $200 million in annualized revenue deals, and is actively repurchasing shares to drive shareholder returns. Operational improvements in employee retention and safety are supporting cost controls and margin gains, while management remains vigilant in navigating regulatory complexities and macro uncertainty, with additional upside possible from further M&A and volume recovery.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Molina signals $46B premium revenue target for 2026 as it revises 2025 EPS floor to $19 amid medical cost pressure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Molina Healthcare (MOH) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Joseph Michael Zubretsky reported "adjusted earnings per share of $5.48 on $10.9 billion of premium revenue" for the second quarter, highlighting that "our 90.4% consolidated MCR reflects a very challenging medical cost trend environment, but moderated by our consistently effective medical cost management." Zubretsky emphasized that Medicaid's MCR reached 91.3%, citing ongoing medical cost pressure in behavioral pharmacy, inpatient, and outpatient care, and noted, "The magnitude and persistence of these medical cost increases are unprecedented."
  • He described significant headwinds in the Marketplace segment, stating, "Marketplace is 10% of our revenue and accounts for nearly half of this 140 basis point MCR revision," and confirmed a revised full year 2025 adjusted EPS guidance of "no less than $19 per share, a floor, if you will, which is $5.50 below our initial guidance of $24.50."
  • Zubretsky outlined growth initiatives, reaffirming the company's premium revenue targets: "We remain on track to achieving our premium revenue target of $46 billion in 2026 and with a modest estimate of future growth initiatives, at least $52 billion for 2027."
  • CFO Mark Lowell Keim stated, "For the quarter, we reported approximately $11 billion in total revenue and $10.9 billion of premium revenue with adjusted EPS of $5.48. Our second quarter consolidated MCR was 90.4%, reflecting a very challenging medical cost trend environment for each of our segments, but moderated by our consistently effective medical cost management."

Outlook

  • Management provided guidance for full year 2025 premium revenue at approximately $42 billion and set a new adjusted EPS floor of $19 per share. Zubretsky explained, "This further revision results from new information gained in our June close process and implications for trend assumptions for the second half of the year, particularly related to Marketplace."
  • The full year consolidated MCR guidance increased to 90.2%, with Marketplace MCR guidance rising from 80% to 85% and Medicaid MCR guidance moving from 89.9% to 90.9%.
  • Zubretsky indicated, "Our outlook considers growth in our current footprint and recent Medicaid and Medicare duals RFP wins. These wins should more than offset the marketplace headwind due to the expiration of enhanced subsidies."

Financial Results

  • Molina reported "adjusted earnings per share of $5.48 on $10.9 billion of premium revenue" for Q2 2025. The adjusted pretax margin was 3.3%, with a consolidated MCR of 90.4%.
  • Medicaid posted an MCR of 91.3%, with continued cost pressure in behavioral, pharmacy, and inpatient/outpatient care. Medicare's MCR was 90%, and the Marketplace MCR was 85.4%, which CFO Keim noted "includes approximately 150 basis points of higher new store MCR in ConnectiCare and 150 basis points for member reconciliation from previous years."
  • The company’s G&A ratio for the quarter was 6.1%, attributed to lower incentive compensation and ongoing productivity enhancements.

Q&A

  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: Questioned confidence in Medicaid margin improvement amidst rate inadequacy and integrity measures. Mark Lowell Keim: Indicated trend "slightly outstripping the rates," but noted prior guidance already factored in rate benefits. "It's all about the relationship of rates and trend, and we already had a lot of rate back in for us."
  • Joshua Richard Raskin, Nephron Research: Asked about the root cause of Marketplace utilization increases. CEO Zubretsky: Stated, "The acuity of the entire marketplace risk pool is higher by 8% year-over-year, which means on a relative basis, risk adjustment is not going to keep up with the elevated trend."
  • Stephen C. Baxter, Wells Fargo: Inquired about Marketplace enrollment decline and acuity shift for 2026. Keim: Emphasized pricing will be state-specific; Zubretsky noted, "Our state-based partners are absolutely willing to give us a second pass rate filing to use the latest information, which should mitigate any mispricing risk."
  • Justin Lake, Wolfe Research: Pressed on run rate earnings and G&A benefit. Keim: Confirmed management compensation benefit was a "onetimer" and likely offset by implementation cost reduction next year.
  • Sarah Elizabeth James, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about ConnectiCare margin trajectory and Marketplace membership. Zubretsky said, "the ConnectiCare acquisition model had us getting to target margins in a 2-year period of 2027."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts exhibited a slightly negative tone, frequently probing the sustainability of margins, rate adequacy, and risk pool acuity, with repeated questions on the drivers of cost pressures and confidence in guidance.
  • Management maintained a measured and cautiously confident tone in prepared remarks but shifted to a more defensive posture during Q&A, illustrated by Zubretsky stating, "We're not going to disclose our rate filing state by state," and repeatedly emphasizing conservatism and mitigation.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management are more cautious. The prior quarter reflected confidence in guidance and growth, while this quarter’s exchanges show more skepticism and defensiveness.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • EPS guidance was revised from at least $24.50 in Q1 to a floor of $19 in Q2, with an explicit $5.50 reduction. Consolidated MCR guidance increased, notably for the Marketplace and Medicaid segments.
  • Management tone shifted from strong confidence in Q1 to caution and emphasis on uncertainty in Q2. Analysts in both quarters focused on cost trends and rate adequacy but pressed harder on risk pool and trend sustainability in Q2.
  • Strategic focus shifted to margin protection, with management reiterating the intent to "prioritize margin and let membership fall where it may" in the Marketplace segment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Persistently rising medical cost trends, especially in behavioral health, pharmacy, and complex inpatient care, were highlighted as unprecedented and challenging.
  • The Marketplace risk pool's increased acuity and uncertainty around the expiration of enhanced subsidies in 2026 were identified as material risks.
  • Management is actively advocating for appropriate rate updates and relies on conservative pricing assumptions to offset risk, while noting that "states are listening and have been responsive."
  • The impact of the recently passed budget bill and future Medicaid membership declines are being closely monitored, but expected to manifest gradually beyond 2028.

Final Takeaway

Molina Healthcare’s second quarter call marked a significant guidance reduction, with the new adjusted EPS floor set at $19 for 2025 amidst accelerating medical cost pressures. Management remains focused on disciplined margin recovery, rate advocacy, and targeted growth, with confidence in long-term revenue targets of $46 billion for 2026 and $52 billion for 2027. The company is actively navigating market volatility and regulatory uncertainty, emphasizing conservative assumptions and capital flexibility as it addresses both near-term headwinds and longer-term opportunities for profitable growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: How long can the Fed and Treasury keep markets inflated?
data.description:
Excess liquidity from a coordinated Federal Reserve and Treasury has been the primary force driving equities, overriding traditional valuation metrics and creating a market distorted by policy, GlobalData.TSLombard said on Thursday. The brokerage questions how long this support can keep markets inflated.  

The analysis argues that quantitative easing (QE) has effectively turned the Fed into the Treasury’s banker, creating a surge in commercial bank deposits that has far outpaced loan growth. This overhang of deposits, which are not being lent out into the broader economy to expand GDP, is instead flowing into and inflating financial markets.

"The 'wall of worry' equities should be climbing is how much longer can liquidity from a Fed/Treasury alignment keep high valuations high. In the very near term, the broad inflow of liquidity will be far less than in the first half of the year," Steven Blitz, Chief U.S. Economist at GlobalData.TSLombard, said in a research note. 

See the chart he shared below:

According to Blitz, warning signs of overvaluation are evident. The economist notes a significant gap between the S&P 500 (as a percentage of domestic product) and corporate profits. The last two times this divergence was so large, during the mid-1960s and the dot-com boom, the subsequent 10-year annualized real returns on equities were negative, at -4% and -6%, respectively.

Furthermore, equity valuations have run far ahead of productivity growth, a disconnect Blitz attributes to the liquidity distortion rather than fundamental economic strength.

In the near term, GlobalData.TSLombard identifies significant risks for the third quarter. The powerful tailwind of liquidity is poised to become a headwind as the Treasury restores $600B in T-bills, foreign capital inflows shrink, and the Fed continues to reduce its balance sheet by $5B per month.

“Overvalued markets are susceptible to downdrafts when liquidity growth falters and macro data stall,” Blitz said.

Looking beyond the immediate headwinds, the bigger question is whether the Fed, potentially under new management, will be pressured to restart QE. Blitz suggests there may be “little choice but to expand the balance sheet to keep real rates in line to generate capex - and help keep equity markets afloat.”

The note concludes that the critical bet for investors is not on whether the market is overvalued but on whether the Fed and Treasury can successfully continue to execute their plan of propping up the capital markets they have inflated.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

And here are some Treasury-linked ETFs: (NASDAQ:TLT), (NYSEARCA:TLH), (NASDAQ:IEF), (NASDAQ:IEI), (NASDAQ:SHY), (NYSEARCA:SGOV), (NYSEARCA:SCHO), and (NYSEARCA:BIL).  

Seeking Alpha editor Kim Khan contributed to this story

data.title: Intel shareholder suit accusing chipmaker of hiding foundry issues tossed
data.description:
A federal judge in California threw out a lawsuit filed by a group of Intel (INTL) shareholders who accused the company of hiding problems related to its foundry business that once revealed, led to a share price plunge.

The suit was initially filed in August 2024, shortly after the chipmaker released its Q2 results. The plaintiffs claim that on Aug. 1, Intel said that the foundry business was "floundering." In an earnings release, Intel said it would engage in a $10B cost reduction plan, lay off more than 15% of its employees, and suspend its dividend later in 2024. On Aug. 2, Intel shares closed down 26%.

The foundry business had an operating loss of $7B in fiscal 2023.

Plaintiffs accused Intel of making misleading or false statements about how the company was doing from Jan. 25 through Aug. 1

However, U.S. District Judge Trina Thompson said that Intel was not liable for delaying the foundry results given that the company said they would be "obscured" and releasing preliminary, unaudited data could have exposed the company to risks, Reuters reported.

data.title: LKQ Corp craters to 5-year low as company shaves FY25 EPS outlook
data.description:
Because of a lack of recovery in auto repairs, tariff uncertainty, general economic softness in Europe, and geopolitical unrest, LKQ Corporation (NASDAQ:LKQ) slashed its FY25 outlook, causing shares to unravel. As a result of a double-digit decline, LKQ (NASDAQ:LKQ) is the worst performing stock on the S&P 500 on Thursday.

Tennessee-based LKQ Corp (NASDAQ:LKQ), which sells replacement parts for automobiles, provides scrap metal to recyclers, and salvage products, now expects organic revenue growth to contract by 1.5% to 3.5% versus prior expectations for growth to be flat to up 2%. Adjusted EPS estimates for the year were lowered to between $2.47 and $2.77 versus prior forecast of $2.91 and $3.21, and nearly $1 less than Wall Street expects at the midpoint.

The company also lowered its free cash flow outlook to $600M to $750M from $750M to $900M, previously, and operating cash flow to a range of $875M to $1.075B from $1.075B to $1.275B.

For the reported quarter, LKQ (LKQ) earned an adjusted profit of $0.87 per share, down from $0.98 in the same quarter last year and $0.31 less than expected. Total revenue was down 2% to an above-consensus $3.64B.

“Our results this quarter reflect a company that is in transformation,” said LKQ CEO Justin Jude. “We will move faster and harder to simplify our business and reduce costs, committed to delivering better results for our customers, employees and partners, and importantly, creating more value for shareholders.”

LKQ Corp (LKQ) shares are down as much as 22% to a nearly 5-year low.

data.title: EC to investigate whether KKR misled on Telecom Italia deal
data.description:
The European Commission opened a formal investigation to determine whether KKR (NYSE:KKR) provided incorrect or misleading information during the EC's merger investigation of KKR's purchase of Telecom Italia's (OTCPK:TIIAY) fixed line network.

The commission unconditionally cleared the acquisition  on May 30, 2024, by concluding that the deal wouldn't raise competition concerns in the European Economic Area. 

The EC studied the acquisition's impact on the market for wholesale broadband access services in Italy. The merger investigation concluded that the transaction wouldn't erode conditions for access to passive services, or terminate such access, because of long-term agreements between FiberCorp (a joint venture between TIM and KKR) and several access seekers, the EC said.

The regulator noted that the opening of a formal investigation doesn't prejudge its outcome.

data.title: STMicroelectronics expects Q3 2025 revenues to rise 14.6% sequentially amid industrial recovery and automotive volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: STMicroelectronics N.V. (STM) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Jean-Marc Chery, CEO, reported "revenues at $2.77 billion, $56 million above the midpoint of our business outlook range, with Automotive slightly below our expectations, which was customer-specific, more than offset by higher revenues in Personal Electronics and Industrial." He emphasized sequential growth in Automotive revenue, especially in Asia Pacific (excluding China and the Americas), and highlighted ongoing execution of the company's car electrification strategy with new design wins for silicon carbide and silicon devices. He also noted strong design-in momentum for automotive-grade sensors and significant wins in MEMS sensors for ADAS, airbag control, and infotainment systems.

  • In Industrial, Chery stated that "Q2 revenues were above expectations with strong sequential growth and continued year-over-year improvement, confirming that Q1 was the bottom." He highlighted a notable recovery in general purpose microcontrollers, now back to year-over-year growth, and announced collaboration with NVIDIA on a new high-power density DC-DC architecture for AI data centers.

  • He reported that the STM32 microcontrollers ecosystem now approaches "1.5 million unique users on a 12-month rolling basis versus the 1.3 million unique users for 2024."

  • Lorenzo Grandi, CFO, stated "gross profit in the second quarter was $926 million, decreasing 28.5% on a year-over-year basis. Gross margin was at 33.5%, decreasing 660 basis points year-over-year, mainly due to unfavorable product mix, lower manufacturing efficiency and, to a lesser extent, higher unused capacity charges." Grandi also reported a $133 million operating loss, which included $190 million for impairment, restructuring, and other phase out costs, reflecting the ongoing program to reshape manufacturing and resize the global cost base. Excluding these nonrecurring items, non-U.S. GAAP operating margin was 2.1% positive.

Outlook

  • Chery guided for Q3 2025 revenues at $3.17 billion, plus/minus 350 basis points. "At the midpoint, our Q3 net revenues will increase 14.6% sequentially and decrease by 2.5% year-over-year with all end markets but Automotive back to year-on-year growth." He expects gross margin to be about 33.5%, plus/minus 200 basis points, including approximately 340 basis points of unused capacity charges and a 140 basis points negative sequential impact mainly from currency effect and nonrecurring costs related to manufacturing reshaping.

  • For the full year 2025, the company plans to maintain its net CapEx plan between $2 billion and $2.3 billion, primarily for manufacturing footprint reshaping.

  • Chery reiterated the target to "deliver annual cost savings in the high triple million dollar range exiting 2027."

Financial Results

  • Analog products, MEMS and Sensors was down 15.2% year-over-year, while MEMS grew double digit. Power and Discrete products decreased 22.2%. Embedded Processing declined 6.5%, and RF & Optical Communications declined 17.9%.

  • Automotive revenue declined by about 24%, Industrial by about 8%, and Personal Electronics and Communication Equipment and Computer Peripherals each declined by about 5% year-over-year. Sequentially, all segments grew, led by Industrial (15%) and Automotive (14%).

  • Q2 net income was a negative $97 million compared to $353 million in the year ago quarter. Diluted earnings per share were a negative $0.11 compared to a positive $0.38. Excluding nonrecurring items, non-U.S. GAAP net income and diluted earnings per share were a positive $57 million and $0.06, respectively.

  • Free cash flow was a negative $152 million in the second quarter compared to a positive $159 million in the year ago quarter. Inventory ended at $3.27 billion with days sales of inventory at 166 days. Net financial position remained solid at $2.67 billion.

Q&A

  • Janardan Menon, Jefferies: Questioned the impact of one-off manufacturing reshaping costs on Q3 gross margin guidance and future gross margin trajectory. Grandi responded that about 20% of the 140 basis points negative impact in Q3 gross margin comes from manufacturing reshaping, offset by a reduction in unused capacity charges and some improvement in manufacturing efficiency, although still suboptimal.

  • Joshua Buchalter, TD Cowen: Asked about changes in customer order patterns amid geopolitical uncertainty and Mobileye foundry relationship. Chery stated that "in Q3, all our, let's say, verticals will grow sequentially and year-over-year, except the Automotive specific to one customer," clarifying that the revenue gap is mostly intangible due to decreased capacity reservation fees. He added there were no observable pull-ins in the June quarter and that revenue from Mobileye is not expected to be impacted for the next 3–5 years.

  • Francois Bouvignies, UBS: Inquired about the year-over-year revenue guidance and cause of the gap since June. Chery explained that "90% of this $80 million gap versus a turning point is, in fact, intangible. It is capacity fee reservation from carmaker that has decreased by $70 million." He emphasized the gap is not a market share loss but a customer-specific adjustment.

  • Sandeep Deshpande, JPMorgan: Asked about Q4 prospects and impact of new U.S. rules on EVs. Chery said "we expect to grow sequentially in Q4," and on silicon carbide, "we confirm that we strongly believe that we can keep 30% market share on this market."

  • Stephane Houri, ODDO BHF: Sought clarification on Q4 gross margin improvement and industrial recovery. Grandi cited a decline in unused capacity charges and improved manufacturing efficiency as key drivers for an expected sequential gross margin increase in Q4. Chery explained industrial recovery is driven by real demand, not inventory replenishment, with POS growing both sequentially and year-over-year.

  • Gianmarco Bonacina, Banca Akros: Asked about gross margin sensitivity to FX and strategy in China. Grandi confirmed negative FX impact but noted expected gross margin leverage as unused capacity charges and manufacturing inefficiencies diminish. Chery and Grandi detailed the "China for China" strategy, with Chinese customers representing approximately 13–14% of total revenues.

  • Lee Simpson, Morgan Stanley: Asked about microcontroller pricing and readiness for the 800-volt PSU project with NVIDIA. Grandi reported stable low single-digit pricing trends, while Marco Cassis stated the 800-volt project is "a work in progress" with no roadblocks foreseen.

  • Sebastien Sztabowicz, Kepler Cheuvreux: Queried about inventory levels and silicon carbide pricing in China. Grandi indicated distribution inventory excess has declined by about one month and continues to normalize. Cassis acknowledged strong price pressure on silicon carbide in China but said new generation products and local manufacturing are being leveraged to remain competitive.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly negative tone, pressing on gross margin sustainability, inventory normalization, and automotive revenue volatility. Questions repeatedly probed the durability of the industrial recovery, the specifics of the automotive revenue gap, and potential market share risks in China and silicon carbide.

  • Management's tone was measured, occasionally defensive, but generally confident in describing sequential recovery, the robustness of the product portfolio, and the ability to manage customer-specific downturns. Phrases such as "we expect to grow sequentially" and "we are confident that we can keep 30% market share" signaled optimism, though explanations for negative trends were detailed and transparent.

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence has increased, with clearer signs of recovery in Industrial and Personal Electronics and a more positive outlook for H2. Analyst skepticism persisted regarding the pace of recovery and the impact of lingering sector headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 revenues rose to $2.77 billion from $2.52 billion in Q1, with sequential growth across all segments, particularly in Industrial and Automotive, reversing previous declines.

  • Automotive revenue, while still below expectations, improved sequentially after Q1 was described as the low point. Customer-specific issues continue to weigh on year-over-year comparisons.

  • Gross margin remained relatively flat (33.5% in Q2 vs. 33.4% in Q1), as anticipated, with unused capacity charges and manufacturing inefficiencies still present but diminishing.

  • Q2 saw a larger net operating loss, largely due to restructuring and impairment costs associated with the manufacturing footprint reshaping. Excluding these, operating margin turned slightly positive.

  • Management's tone shifted from cautious to more confident, particularly regarding industrial recovery and the normalization of inventory levels. Analysts maintained a focus on gross margin, inventory, and competitive risks, especially in China.

Risks and Concerns

  • Uncertainty persists in the Automotive segment due to customer-specific demand fluctuations and competitive instability driven by geopolitical and market dynamics.

  • Management cited ongoing risks from global trade tariffs and currency fluctuations, explicitly noting the impact on gross margin and the need to continue monitoring regulatory shifts.

  • Price pressure in China, especially for silicon carbide, remains a significant challenge, with the company relying on product innovation and local manufacturing to mitigate risk.

  • Analysts remain vigilant about inventory normalization, the durability of the industrial upcycle, and the sustainability of cost savings from the manufacturing reshaping program.

Final Takeaway

STMicroelectronics delivered sequential revenue growth and signaled a turning point in Industrial and Personal Electronics, while Automotive performance continues to be affected by customer-specific factors and capacity reservation fee reductions. The company anticipates robust Q3 sequential growth and expects gross margin improvement in Q4 as cost controls and manufacturing efficiency gains take effect. Management reiterates its commitment to cost savings, manufacturing reshaping, and maintaining a strong position in silicon carbide, with particular attention to risks in China and the ongoing volatility in the Automotive market.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
